u3_l3.2.title: Measures of Spread (range, variance, standard deviation)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Measures of Spread (range, variance, standard deviation)
summary_sections.content_html:
Measures of Spread

Spread (variability) measures describe how spread out or dispersed data values are around the center.

  • Range: Maximum − Minimum. Simple but sensitive to outliers.
  • Variance (s²): The average of the squared deviations from the mean. Uses n−1 in the denominator for sample variance (Bessel's correction).
  • Standard Deviation (s): The square root of the variance. It is in the same units as the data and measures typical distance from the mean.
  • IQR (Interquartile Range): Q3 − Q1. Resistant to outliers; measures spread of the middle 50%.

A low standard deviation means data are clustered near the mean; a high standard deviation means data are widely spread.

The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 rule) for approximately normal distributions: about 68% of data fall within 1 SD of the mean, 95% within 2 SD, and 99.7% within 3 SD.

flashcards.definition: The difference between the maximum and minimum values in a dataset: Range = Max − Min.
The average of squared deviations from the mean; for a sample, divide by n − 1.
The square root of the variance; measures the typical distance of data points from the mean, in the same units as the data.
For approximately normal distributions: ~68% of data within 1 SD, ~95% within 2 SD, ~99.7% within 3 SD of the mean.
Using n − 1 instead of n in the denominator of sample variance to produce an unbiased estimate of the population variance.

quiz_questions.question_text: The range of {5, 12, 8, 3, 20} is:
Standard deviation measures:

options.text: The typical distance of data points from the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard deviation quantifies how spread out data are around the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: Variance is the:

options.text: Square root of the standard deviation
Average of squared deviations from the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: Variance = Σ(x − x̄)² / (n − 1) for a sample.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do we use n−1 in sample variance?

options.text: To produce an unbiased estimate of the population variance (Bessel's correction)

quiz_questions.explanation: Dividing by n−1 corrects for the bias that arises from estimating the mean from the sample itself.

quiz_questions.question_text: A low standard deviation means:

options.text: Data are clustered close to the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: A small SD indicates that most data points are near the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to the Empirical Rule, about 95% of data fall within:
quiz_questions.explanation: The Empirical Rule states approximately 95% of data lie within 2 standard deviations of the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Empirical Rule applies to:
quiz_questions.explanation: The 68-95-99.7 rule is specifically for approximately normal distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: The IQR is resistant to outliers because:

options.text: It only considers the middle 50% of data (Q1 to Q3)

quiz_questions.explanation: The IQR measures spread between the 25th and 75th percentiles, unaffected by extreme values.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the mean is 50 and the SD is 5, approximately 68% of data lie between:
The range is sensitive to outliers because:

options.text: It depends only on the maximum and minimum values

quiz_questions.explanation: One extreme value can dramatically change the range.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the data {10, 10, 10, 10, 10}, the standard deviation is:
quiz_questions.explanation: When all values are the same, there is no spread — SD = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which has a larger standard deviation: {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} or {1, 1, 3, 5, 5}?
quiz_questions.explanation: The second set has values further from the mean (3), producing a larger SD.

quiz_questions.question_text: Population standard deviation uses __ in the denominator:
quiz_questions.explanation: Population SD divides by N; sample SD divides by n − 1.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you add a constant to every value in a dataset, the standard deviation:
quiz_questions.explanation: Adding a constant shifts all values equally, leaving spread unchanged.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you multiply every value by 2, the standard deviation:
quiz_questions.explanation: Multiplying by a constant scales the SD by the same factor.

quiz_questions.question_text: The variance of a dataset is always:
quiz_questions.explanation: Variance is a sum of squared deviations, so it is always ≥ 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which measure of spread is in the same units as the data?
quiz_questions.explanation: SD is the square root of variance, returning to the original units.

quiz_questions.question_text: Within 3 standard deviations of the mean, approximately what percentage of data fall (Empirical Rule)?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Empirical Rule says ~99.7% of data fall within 3 SDs of the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: To compare spreads of two datasets with different units, use:
quiz_questions.explanation: The CV expresses SD as a percentage of the mean, allowing unit-free comparison.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a teacher's test scores have SD = 0, this means:
quiz_questions.explanation: SD = 0 means zero variability — all values are identical.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Percentiles & Quartiles
summary_sections.content_html:
Percentiles & Quartiles

Percentiles divide a dataset into 100 equal parts. The k-th percentile is the value below which k% of the data fall.

  • 25th percentile (Q1): 25% of data fall below this value.
  • 50th percentile (Q2 / Median): 50% of data fall below.
  • 75th percentile (Q3): 75% of data fall below.

Quartiles divide data into four quarters:

  • Q1 separates the lowest 25%.
  • Q2 (median) separates the lower and upper halves.
  • Q3 separates the lowest 75%.

Percentiles are widely used in standardized testing (e.g., 'You scored in the 90th percentile means you scored higher than 90% of test-takers'), growth charts, and income reporting.

The percentile rank of a value tells you what percentage of data falls at or below that value.

flashcards.definition: A value below which a given percentage of data falls; the k-th percentile has k% of data below it.
One of three values (Q1, Q2, Q3) that divide an ordered dataset into four equal parts.
The 25th percentile; 25% of the data fall below this value.
The 75th percentile; 75% of the data fall below this value.
The percentage of values in a dataset that fall at or below a given value.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 50th percentile is the same as:
quiz_questions.explanation: The 50th percentile divides the data in half, which defines the median.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a test score is at the 85th percentile, the student scored higher than:
quiz_questions.explanation: The 85th percentile means 85% of scores fall below that value.

quiz_questions.question_text: Q1 is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Q1 corresponds to the 25th percentile.

quiz_questions.question_text: Q3 is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Q3 is the 75th percentile.

quiz_questions.question_text: The IQR equals:
quiz_questions.explanation: IQR = Q3 − Q1, the range of the middle 50% of data.

quiz_questions.question_text: A baby at the 60th percentile for weight:

options.text: Weighs more than 60% of babies the same age

quiz_questions.explanation: The 60th percentile means the baby is heavier than 60% of peers.

quiz_questions.question_text: Percentiles divide data into:
quiz_questions.explanation: Percentiles create 100 equal divisions of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: The percentile rank of a value is:

options.text: The percentage of data at or below that value

quiz_questions.explanation: Percentile rank tells you what portion of the data is at or below a given value.

quiz_questions.question_text: If n = 20 and a value is greater than 15 of the 20 observations, its approximate percentile rank is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Percentile rank ≈ (15/20) × 100 = 75%.

quiz_questions.question_text: Quartiles divide data into:
quiz_questions.explanation: The three quartiles split data into four equal quarters.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student scoring at the 99th percentile:

options.text: Scored higher than 99% of all test-takers

quiz_questions.explanation: 99th percentile means 99% of scores are at or below that student's score.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which percentile is Q2?
quiz_questions.explanation: Q2 is the median, which is the 50th percentile.

quiz_questions.question_text: Deciles divide data into:
quiz_questions.explanation: Deciles create 10 equal groups: 10th, 20th, …, 90th percentiles.

quiz_questions.question_text: If Q1 = 30 and Q3 = 70, the IQR is:
The 0th percentile would be:

options.text: The minimum or below all data

quiz_questions.explanation: The 0th percentile represents the lowest possible position — at or below all data.

quiz_questions.question_text: Standardized test reports often use percentiles because:

options.text: They show how a student compares to all test-takers

quiz_questions.explanation: Percentiles communicate relative standing among all test-takers.

quiz_questions.question_text: If two students are at the 80th and 90th percentiles, you can say:

options.text: The difference in their scores is exactly 10 points
The second student outperformed a higher proportion of test-takers

quiz_questions.explanation: Percentiles indicate relative position, not exact score differences.

quiz_questions.question_text: A value at the 100th percentile:

options.text: Is at or above all values in the dataset

quiz_questions.explanation: The 100th percentile is the maximum value.

quiz_questions.question_text: Growth charts for children use percentiles to:

options.text: Track physical growth relative to peers of the same age

quiz_questions.explanation: Pediatric growth charts use percentiles to compare a child's growth to a reference population.

quiz_questions.question_text: If your salary is at the 40th percentile, this means:

options.text: You earn 40% of the maximum salary
40% of people earn the same or less than you

quiz_questions.explanation: 40th percentile means 40% of the comparison group earns at or below your level.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Z-Scores & Standardization
summary_sections.content_html:
Z-Scores & Standardization

A z-score (standard score) tells you how many standard deviations a data value is from the mean.

Formula: z = (x − x̄) / s

  • z = 0 means the value equals the mean.
  • z > 0 means the value is above the mean.
  • z < 0 means the value is below the mean.
  • z = 2 means the value is 2 standard deviations above the mean.

Standardization converts data to z-scores, creating a distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1. This allows comparison of values from different datasets with different units or scales.

Example: Who performed better — a student with a score of 85 (class mean 75, SD 5) or a score of 90 (class mean 82, SD 6)? Their z-scores: (85−75)/5 = 2.0 vs. (90−82)/6 ≈ 1.33. The first student is relatively higher.

Z-scores are also used to identify unusual values: typically, |z| > 2 is considered unusual, and |z| > 3 is very unusual (potential outlier).

flashcards.definition: The number of standard deviations a data value is from the mean: z = (x − x̄) / s.
Converting data values to z-scores, producing a distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1.
A normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1, resulting from standardizing data.
A data point with |z| > 2, meaning it lies more than 2 standard deviations from the mean.
Z-scores allow fair comparison of values from different distributions by putting them on the same scale.

quiz_questions.question_text: The z-score formula is:
quiz_questions.explanation: z = (x − mean) / standard deviation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A z-score of 0 means:
quiz_questions.explanation: z = 0 indicates that the value is exactly at the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: A z-score of −1.5 means the value is:
quiz_questions.explanation: A negative z-score means the value is below the mean by that many SDs.

quiz_questions.question_text: If x̄ = 100 and s = 10, what is the z-score for x = 120?
A z-score of 2.5 is considered:

options.text: Unusual (more than 2 SDs from the mean)

quiz_questions.explanation: |z| > 2 is generally considered unusual.

quiz_questions.question_text: Standardization produces a distribution with:
quiz_questions.explanation: Standardized data always have mean 0 and standard deviation 1.

quiz_questions.question_text: Z-scores allow you to:

options.text: Compare values from different distributions on a common scale

quiz_questions.explanation: Z-scores put different datasets on the same scale for fair comparison.

quiz_questions.question_text: Student A: score 88, mean 80, SD 4. Student B: score 92, mean 86, SD 3. Who performed better relative to their class?

options.text: Student A (z = 2.0 vs. z = 2.0 — both the same!)

quiz_questions.explanation: z_A = (88−80)/4 = 2.0; z_B = (92−86)/3 = 2.0. They performed equally well relative to their classes.

quiz_questions.question_text: If z = 3, about what percentage of data in a normal distribution lies above this value?
quiz_questions.explanation: By the Empirical Rule, 99.7% lies within 3 SDs, so only about 0.15% lies above z = 3.

quiz_questions.question_text: Converting all data values to z-scores changes the:

options.text: Data values but not their relative positions
Scale (units) but keeps relative positions and shape

quiz_questions.explanation: Standardization rescales data but preserves the shape and relative positions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A negative z-score always means:

options.text: The value is below the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative z-scores indicate values that are less than the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: The z-score is unitless because:

options.text: Subtracting and dividing by values in the same units cancels the units

quiz_questions.explanation: The units cancel when you subtract the mean and divide by the SD.

quiz_questions.question_text: If x̄ = 70 and s = 8, what value has a z-score of −2?
For a normal distribution, approximately what percentage of z-scores fall between −1 and 1?
quiz_questions.explanation: By the Empirical Rule, about 68% of data fall within 1 SD (z between −1 and 1).

quiz_questions.question_text: An observation with z = 0.5 is:
quiz_questions.explanation: z = 0.5 means half a standard deviation above the mean — slightly above average.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students take different exams. The ONLY way to fairly compare their performances is by using:
quiz_questions.explanation: Z-scores account for different means and SDs across exams.

quiz_questions.question_text: If s = 0, z-scores:
quiz_questions.explanation: If SD = 0, all values are identical and z-scores can't be computed.

quiz_questions.question_text: The z-score corresponding to the mean is always:
In a standard normal distribution:
quiz_questions.explanation: The standard normal has mean 0 and SD 1 by definition.

quiz_questions.question_text: A z-score of −3 would suggest the observation is:

options.text: Very unusual — far below the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: |z| = 3 is very unusual, lying in the extreme tail of the distribution.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Coefficient of Variation
summary_sections.content_html:
Coefficient of Variation

The Coefficient of Variation (CV) expresses the standard deviation as a percentage of the mean, allowing comparison of variability between datasets with different units or scales.

Formula: CV = (s / x̄) × 100%

  • A higher CV indicates greater relative variability.
  • A lower CV indicates less relative variability (more consistent data).
  • The CV is unitless, making it ideal for comparing variability across datasets measured in different units.

Example: Dataset A has mean 50, SD 10 → CV = 20%. Dataset B has mean 200, SD 30 → CV = 15%. Although B has a larger SD, it has less relative variability.

Limitations: The CV is not meaningful when the mean is zero or near zero, or for data measured on an interval scale (like temperature in °C, where 0 is not a true zero).

flashcards.definition: The ratio of the standard deviation to the mean, expressed as a percentage: CV = (s / x̄) × 100%.
Variability expressed in proportion to the mean, allowing fair comparisons between datasets of different scales.
A statistic with no units, enabling comparison across datasets measured in different units.
Indicates greater relative spread or inconsistency in the data relative to its mean.
Indicates less relative spread — data are more consistent relative to their mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: The CV formula is:
quiz_questions.explanation: CV = (standard deviation / mean) × 100%.

quiz_questions.question_text: The CV is useful when comparing:

options.text: Datasets with the same units and scale
Datasets with different units or scales

quiz_questions.explanation: The CV's unitless ratio allows comparisons across different scales.

quiz_questions.question_text: Dataset A: mean 40, SD 8. CV = ?
Dataset B: mean 200, SD 20. CV = ?
Between Dataset A (CV=20%) and Dataset B (CV=10%), which is more consistent?
quiz_questions.explanation: A lower CV means less relative variability — Dataset B is more consistent.

quiz_questions.question_text: The CV is unitless because:

options.text: The units cancel when dividing SD by the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: Since SD and mean have the same units, dividing cancels them out.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is the CV NOT meaningful?

options.text: When the mean is zero or near zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Division by zero (or near zero) makes the CV undefined or misleadingly large.

quiz_questions.question_text: A quality control team finds CV = 2% for product weights. This suggests:
quiz_questions.explanation: A CV of 2% indicates that the SD is only 2% of the mean — very consistent.

quiz_questions.question_text: If two machines produce bolts — Machine X (CV = 5%) vs. Machine Y (CV = 12%):
quiz_questions.explanation: A lower CV (Machine X) means less relative variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: The CV should not be used with interval-scale data like temperature in °C because:

options.text: 0°C is not a true zero; the ratio SD/mean isn't meaningful
The CV formula doesn't accept negatives

quiz_questions.explanation: The CV requires a ratio-level scale with a true zero for the ratio to be meaningful.

quiz_questions.question_text: Company A has mean revenue $1M, SD $200K. Company B has mean $10M, SD $500K. Which has more relative variability?
quiz_questions.explanation: CV_A = 200K/1M = 20%; CV_B = 500K/10M = 5%. Company A has more relative variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: The CV can be compared across datasets because:

options.text: It is always a whole number

quiz_questions.explanation: Being unitless allows the CV to compare variability across any pair of datasets.

quiz_questions.question_text: A high CV in test scores suggests:
quiz_questions.explanation: A high CV indicates scores are widely spread relative to the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: If every value in a dataset is multiplied by 5, the CV:
quiz_questions.explanation: Both the mean and SD multiply by 5, so their ratio (CV) stays the same.

quiz_questions.question_text: CV is expressed as:
quiz_questions.explanation: The CV is reported as a percentage.

quiz_questions.question_text: For comparing the spread of heights (cm) and weights (kg), you should use:
quiz_questions.explanation: Since heights and weights have different units, the CV enables a fair comparison.

quiz_questions.question_text: If x̄ = 0 and s = 5, the CV is:
quiz_questions.explanation: CV = s/x̄ = 5/0, which is undefined.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why might you prefer the CV over just comparing standard deviations?

options.text: SD alone doesn't account for differences in the magnitude of the mean
The CV is easier to compute

quiz_questions.explanation: Two datasets may have different SDs simply because their means differ; the CV normalizes for scale.

quiz_questions.question_text: A CV of 0% means:

options.text: There is no variability — all values are identical

quiz_questions.explanation: CV = 0 when SD = 0, meaning every value equals the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: The population CV uses:
quiz_questions.explanation: The population CV uses the population SD (σ) and population mean (μ).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Interpreting Data in Context
summary_sections.content_html:
Interpreting Data in Context

Statistical values are meaningless without context. Interpreting data means connecting numerical results back to the real-world situation they describe.

  • Context includes: Who or what was studied, what was measured, when and where the data were collected, and why the study was done.
  • Interpreting the mean: 'The average commute time for employees at Company X is 28 minutes' — not just 'x̄ = 28.'
  • Interpreting the SD: 'Commute times typically vary by about 12 minutes from the average' — not just 's = 12.'
  • Interpreting a z-score: 'A commute of 52 minutes is 2 SDs above the mean, which is unusually long compared to peers.'

Always answer in complete sentences that reference the variable, units, and context. Avoid naked numbers.

Common interpretations on AP exams:

  • Mean: 'On average, [variable] is [value] [units].'
  • SD: '[Variable] values typically differ from the mean by about [value] [units].'
  • Percentile: '[Value] is at the [k]th percentile, meaning [k]% of [subjects] have a [variable] at or below [value].'

flashcards.definition: The real-world background (who, what, when, where, why) that gives meaning to numerical results.
Explaining a statistical result in plain language that connects the number to its real-world meaning, including variable names and units.
The measurement context (e.g., minutes, dollars, kilograms) that must be included when interpreting statistics.
A statistical result presented without context or explanation — to be avoided.
A complete sentence connecting a statistic to its context: 'On average, [variable] is [value] [units].'

quiz_questions.question_text: Why must statistics be interpreted in context?

options.text: Numbers alone are meaningless without knowing what they represent

quiz_questions.explanation: Without knowing what was measured, numbers carry no real-world meaning.

quiz_questions.question_text: A complete interpretation of x̄ = 72 should include:

options.text: What was measured, the units, and who was studied

quiz_questions.explanation: A full interpretation references the variable, units, and subjects.

quiz_questions.question_text: 's = 4.5 inches' for student heights means:

options.text: Heights typically differ from the mean by about 4.5 inches

quiz_questions.explanation: The SD measures typical deviation from the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'The median household income is $58,000' tells us:

options.text: Half of households earn less than $58,000 and half earn more

quiz_questions.explanation: The median splits the income distribution in half.

quiz_questions.question_text: On an AP exam, a 'naked number' answer would be:
quiz_questions.explanation: A naked number lacks context and will not receive full credit on AP exams.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'A z-score of 1.8 for this exam score' should be interpreted as:

options.text: The score is 1.8 standard deviations above the class mean

quiz_questions.explanation: Z-scores express distance from the mean in standard-deviation units.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'The IQR of commute times is 15 minutes' means:

options.text: The middle 50% of commute times span 15 minutes

quiz_questions.explanation: The IQR is the range of the middle 50% of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which interpretation is best? 'r = 0.85':

options.text: 'There is a strong positive linear relationship between study hours and exam scores'

quiz_questions.explanation: Interpretation must name the variables and describe the relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: Context answers which questions?

options.text: Who, what, when, where, and why

quiz_questions.explanation: Full context addresses all relevant aspects of the study.

quiz_questions.question_text: '95% confidence interval for the mean is (48, 52)' should be interpreted as:

options.text: We are 95% confident the true population mean lies between 48 and 52

quiz_questions.explanation: Confidence intervals estimate the range likely containing the population parameter.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a report states 'SD = 3.2,' a reader should ask:

options.text: '3.2 of what? What variable? What units?'

quiz_questions.explanation: Without context, 3.2 is meaningless — the variable and units are essential.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'The percentage of adults who exercise daily is 23%' is interpretable because:

options.text: It specifies who (adults), what (exercise daily), and the finding (23%)

quiz_questions.explanation: The sentence provides context: the group, the behavior, and the result.

quiz_questions.question_text: When interpreting a boxplot comparison, mention:

options.text: Differences in center (median), spread (IQR), and any outliers, in context

quiz_questions.explanation: A complete comparison references center, spread, outliers, and what the data represent.

quiz_questions.question_text: Describing a distribution requires noting:

options.text: Shape, center, spread, and unusual features — all in context

quiz_questions.explanation: A thorough description addresses all aspects and connects them to the real-world variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'The correlation is −0.72' should be stated as:

options.text: 'There is a moderately strong negative linear relationship between [x] and [y]'

quiz_questions.explanation: Interpretation must name the variables and describe the nature of the relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: An AP exam grader looks for:

options.text: Correct computations AND interpretations in context

quiz_questions.explanation: AP scoring requires both correct numerical answers and contextual interpretation.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'On average, customers wait 7.5 minutes for service' is:
quiz_questions.explanation: It states the variable (wait time), the subjects (customers), and the result (7.5 min).

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is including units important?

options.text: Units specify the scale of measurement, making the number meaningful

quiz_questions.explanation: Units tell the reader what was measured and in what scale.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is a better AP-style answer for a percentile?

options.text: 'A score of 650 is at the 90th percentile, meaning the student scored higher than 90% of test-takers'

quiz_questions.explanation: The full sentence names the variable, the percentile, and its meaning.

quiz_questions.question_text: Interpreting data in context is important because:

options.text: It connects statistical results to real-world meaning, enabling informed decisions

quiz_questions.explanation: Context transforms numbers into actionable insights.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Sports & Medicine
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Sports & Medicine

Descriptive statistics play a critical role in both sports analytics and medical research.

Sports Analytics:

  • Batting averages, ERA, player efficiency ratings are all descriptive statistics used to evaluate performance.
  • Moneyball: The Oakland A's used statistical analysis (OBP, SLG) rather than traditional scouting to build a competitive team on a small budget. This revolutionized baseball and spread to other sports.
  • Modern sports use tracking data (player movement, speed, distance covered) to optimize training and strategy.

Medical Research:

  • Clinical trials use means, medians, and survival rates to compare treatments.
  • Vital statistics: Life expectancy, infant mortality rate, and disease prevalence are descriptive measures reported by public health agencies.
  • BMI (Body Mass Index): A widely used (though debated) descriptive measure: weight(kg) / height(m)².
  • Epidemiological studies: Track disease incidence (new cases) and prevalence (total cases) to guide public health policy.

flashcards.definition: The use of advanced statistical analysis (sabermetrics) by the Oakland A's to evaluate players and build a competitive team on a limited budget.
A descriptive statistic in baseball: the number of hits divided by the number of at-bats.
A controlled medical experiment comparing treatments using descriptive and inferential statistics.
A descriptive measure of body composition: weight (kg) / height (m)², used in health screening.
Incidence: the rate of new disease cases. Prevalence: the total proportion of a population with the disease at a given time.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the Moneyball approach, the Oakland A's relied on:

options.text: Statistical analysis to find undervalued players

quiz_questions.explanation: Moneyball used statistics (OBP, SLG) to identify players others overlooked.

quiz_questions.question_text: A batting average is calculated as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Batting average = total hits divided by total at-bats.

quiz_questions.question_text: In clinical trials, the median survival time is preferred over the mean because:

options.text: Survival data are often right-skewed with outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: Survival data often have a long right tail, making the median more representative.

quiz_questions.question_text: BMI is calculated as:
quiz_questions.explanation: BMI = mass in kilograms divided by the square of height in meters.

quiz_questions.question_text: Disease incidence refers to:

options.text: The rate of new cases occurring in a population over a time period

quiz_questions.explanation: Incidence measures how many new cases arise.

quiz_questions.question_text: Disease prevalence refers to:

options.text: The total proportion of a population with the disease at a given time

quiz_questions.explanation: Prevalence counts all existing cases, both new and ongoing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Player efficiency rating (PER) in basketball is a type of:

options.text: Descriptive statistic summarizing overall player performance

quiz_questions.explanation: PER summarizes a player's contributions — a descriptive measure.

quiz_questions.question_text: Modern sports analytics use tracking data to:

options.text: Optimize training, strategy, and injury prevention

quiz_questions.explanation: Tracking data help teams fine-tune performance and reduce injuries.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a study comparing Drug A vs. placebo, the mean blood pressure reduction in each group is an example of:

options.text: A descriptive statistic used to compare groups

quiz_questions.explanation: The mean reduction summarizes each group's response — descriptive statistics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Life expectancy is a descriptive statistic reported by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Life expectancy is a population-level descriptive measure tracked by public health agencies.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is BMI considered debated?

options.text: It doesn't distinguish between muscle and fat, and may misclassify fit individuals

quiz_questions.explanation: BMI doesn't account for body composition — muscular individuals may be classified as overweight.

quiz_questions.question_text: OBP (On-Base Percentage) measures:

options.text: How frequently a batter reaches base

quiz_questions.explanation: OBP = (hits + walks + HBP) / (at-bats + walks + HBP + sac flies).

quiz_questions.question_text: In medical research, a control group that receives a placebo helps:

options.text: Provide a baseline to measure the drug's actual effect

quiz_questions.explanation: The control group's response provides a comparison for the treatment group.

quiz_questions.question_text: The infant mortality rate is a:

options.text: Descriptive statistic measuring deaths of infants per 1,000 live births

quiz_questions.explanation: It's a descriptive measure of infant deaths relative to births.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sports analytics became mainstream after:
quiz_questions.explanation: Moneyball popularized the use of statistical analysis in sports decision-making.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sabermetrics is:

options.text: The empirical analysis of baseball through statistics

quiz_questions.explanation: Sabermetrics applies statistical methods to analyze baseball performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A study reports 'mean cholesterol decreased by 15 mg/dL in the treatment group.' This is:

options.text: A descriptive result interpreted in medical context

quiz_questions.explanation: The mean decrease summarizes the treatment effect in meaningful units.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tracking player distance covered per game helps teams:

options.text: Assess fitness levels and manage workload to prevent injury

quiz_questions.explanation: Workload tracking informs training decisions and injury prevention.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why should medical studies report both mean and median?

options.text: They provide different perspectives; the median is resistant to outliers in skewed data

quiz_questions.explanation: Reporting both gives a fuller picture, especially when data are skewed.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Basic Probability Rules
summary_sections.content_html:
Basic Probability Rules

Probability measures the likelihood of an event occurring, expressed as a number from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).

  • Sample space (S): The set of all possible outcomes.
  • Event (E): A subset of the sample space.
  • P(E) = Number of favorable outcomes / Total number of outcomes (for equally likely outcomes).
  • Complement rule: P(E') = 1 − P(E). The probability of an event NOT happening.
  • Addition rule (mutually exclusive): P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) when A and B cannot both occur.
  • General addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B).
  • Multiplication rule (independent): P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) when events are independent.

Probabilities can be expressed as fractions, decimals, or percentages. The sum of probabilities of all outcomes in a sample space is 1.

flashcards.definition: The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment (denoted S).
A number between 0 and 1 measuring the likelihood of an event, where 0 = impossible and 1 = certain.
P(E') = 1 − P(E); the probability of the event NOT occurring.
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B); for mutually exclusive events, P(A and B) = 0.
P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) when events A and B do not affect each other.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Counting Principles (permutations, combinations)
summary_sections.content_html:
Counting Principles

Counting principles determine the number of ways to arrange or select items, which is essential for calculating probabilities.

  • Fundamental Counting Principle: If event A can occur in m ways and event B in n ways, then A followed by B can occur in m × n ways.
  • Permutation: An ordered arrangement of objects. The number of permutations of n objects taken r at a time: P(n, r) = n! / (n − r)!
  • Combination: A selection where order does NOT matter. C(n, r) = n! / [r!(n − r)!]
  • Factorial: n! = n × (n−1) × (n−2) × … × 1. By definition, 0! = 1.

Key distinction: Use permutations when order matters (e.g., rankings, codes), combinations when order doesn't matter (e.g., committees, groups).

flashcards.definition: If one event can occur in m ways and a second in n ways, the events together can occur in m × n ways.
An ordered arrangement of objects; P(n,r) = n!/(n−r)!.
A selection of objects where order does not matter; C(n,r) = n!/[r!(n−r)!].

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Independent & Dependent Events
summary_sections.content_html:
Independent & Dependent Events

Understanding whether events are independent or dependent is critical for calculating probabilities correctly.

  • Independent events: The occurrence of one event does NOT affect the probability of the other. Example: Flipping a coin twice — the outcome of the first flip doesn't affect the second.
  • Dependent events: The occurrence of one event DOES affect the probability of the other. Example: Drawing two cards from a deck without replacement — the first draw changes the remaining cards.

Test for independence: Events A and B are independent if and only if P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B), or equivalently P(A|B) = P(A).

Multiplication rule:

  • Independent: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B)
  • Dependent: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A)

'With replacement' typically makes events independent; 'without replacement' makes them dependent.

flashcards.definition: Two events where the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).
Two events where the occurrence of one changes the probability of the other.
After an item is selected, it is returned to the pool before the next selection, maintaining independence.
After an item is selected, it is NOT returned, changing probabilities for subsequent selections.
P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A), where P(B|A) is the probability of B given A has occurred.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Conditional Probability
summary_sections.content_html:
Conditional Probability

Conditional probability is the probability of an event occurring given that another event has already occurred.

Formula: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B), where P(B) > 0.

Read 'P(A|B)' as 'the probability of A given B.'

  • The '|' symbol means 'given that' — it restricts the sample space to only the outcomes where B occurred.
  • P(A|B) ≠ P(B|A) in general — the order matters.
  • P(A|B) = P(A) only if A and B are independent.

Bayes' Theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B). This allows you to reverse conditional probabilities.

Example: In a group of 100 students, 30 play sports and 20 of those also play music. P(music | sports) = 20/30 = 2/3.

flashcards.definition: The probability of event A occurring given that event B has already occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).
A condition that restricts the sample space to only those outcomes where the given event has occurred.
P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B); used to reverse conditional probabilities.
These are generally NOT equal; the order in conditional probability matters.
In conditional probability, the sample space shrinks to only the outcomes consistent with the given condition.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Probability Trees
summary_sections.content_html:
Probability Trees

A probability tree (tree diagram) is a visual tool for organizing and computing probabilities of sequential events.

  • Each branch represents a possible outcome, labeled with its probability.
  • Branches from the same node must have probabilities that sum to 1.
  • To find P(a specific sequence): multiply the probabilities along the path.
  • To find P(an event that can occur via multiple paths): add the probabilities of all paths leading to that event.

Probability trees are especially helpful for:

  • Multi-stage experiments (e.g., drawing cards, flipping coins, medical testing).
  • Problems involving conditional probability (branches after the first split may have updated probabilities).
  • Visualizing all possible outcomes systematically.

flashcards.definition: A diagram showing all possible outcomes and their probabilities in sequential events, using branches from nodes.

To find the probability of a specific sequence, multiply the probabilities of each branch along that path.
To find the total probability of an event occurring via multiple routes, add the probabilities of all paths leading to it.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Venn Diagrams
summary_sections.content_html:
Venn Diagrams in Probability

A Venn diagram uses overlapping circles within a rectangle to display relationships between events.

  • The rectangle represents the entire sample space (S).
  • Each circle represents an event.
  • The overlap (intersection) represents outcomes in both events: A ∩ B.
  • The union (everything in either circle) represents A ∪ B.

Using Venn diagrams to find probabilities:

  • P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B) → corresponds to the total area of both circles.
  • P(A only) = P(A) − P(A and B) → the part of A's circle not in the overlap.
  • P(neither A nor B) = 1 − P(A or B) → the area outside both circles.

For mutually exclusive events, the circles do not overlap (intersection is empty).

flashcards.definition: A diagram using overlapping circles within a rectangle to visually represent events and their relationships.
The set of outcomes common to both events A and B; the overlapping region in a Venn diagram.
The set of outcomes in event A, event B, or both; the total area covered by the circles.
All outcomes NOT in event A; the area outside A's circle.
Events whose circles do not overlap because they share no common outcomes.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Games of Chance
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Games of Chance

Probability theory originated from analyzing games of chance. Understanding these applications reinforces fundamental concepts.

  • Dice: A fair six-sided die has P(any face) = 1/6. Two dice → 36 equally likely outcomes. P(sum = 7) = 6/36 = 1/6.
  • Cards: A standard deck has 52 cards (4 suits × 13 ranks). P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13.
  • Coins: P(heads) = 1/2 for a fair coin. n flips → 2ⁿ total outcomes.
  • Lottery: Lotteries involve combinations — e.g., choosing 6 numbers from 49 → C(49,6) = 13,983,816 combinations. P(winning) ≈ 1 in 14 million.
  • Expected value: The long-run average outcome. E(X) = Σ[x · P(x)]. Casino games always have a negative expected value for the player (house edge).
  • Gambler's fallacy: The mistaken belief that past random outcomes affect future ones (e.g., 'the coin landed heads 5 times, so tails is due').

flashcards.definition: The long-run average outcome of a random variable: E(X) = Σ[x · P(x)].
The mathematical advantage the casino has over players, ensuring the casino profits in the long run.
The incorrect belief that past outcomes of independent random events affect future outcomes.
Outcomes that all have the same probability of occurring, such as each face of a fair die.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Probability in Genetics
summary_sections.content_html:
Probability in Genetics

Genetics was one of the earliest applications of probability. Gregor Mendel's work in the 1860s used probability principles to explain inheritance patterns.

  • Punnett Square: A grid used to predict genotype ratios of offspring. It is essentially a probability table based on the multiplication rule.
  • Genotype: The genetic composition (e.g., Bb = heterozygous). Phenotype: The observable trait (e.g., brown eyes).
  • Dominant allele (B): Expressed in both BB and Bb genotypes. Recessive allele (b): Only expressed in bb genotype.
  • Monohybrid cross (Bb × Bb): Probability of BB = 1/4, Bb = 1/2, bb = 1/4. Phenotype ratio: 3 dominant : 1 recessive.
  • Independent assortment: Genes on different chromosomes are inherited independently — allowing use of the multiplication rule.
  • Hardy-Weinberg principle: In a large population with no evolutionary forces, allele frequencies remain constant: p² + 2pq + q² = 1.

flashcards.definition: A grid used to predict the probability of offspring genotypes by combining parent alleles.
Genotype is the genetic makeup (e.g., Bb); phenotype is the observable trait (e.g., brown eyes).
An allele expressed whenever present (in Bb or BB); represented by a capital letter.
An allele expressed only when homozygous (bb); represented by a lowercase letter.
A principle stating that allele and genotype frequencies remain constant in a large, randomly mating population without evolutionary forces: p² + 2pq + q² = 1.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Discrete Random Variables
summary_sections.content_html:
Discrete Random Variables

A random variable (X) assigns a numerical value to each outcome in a sample space. A discrete random variable takes a countable number of distinct values (0, 1, 2, …).

  • Probability distribution: A table or function listing every value x and its probability P(X = x).
  • Requirements: (1) 0 ≤ P(X = x) ≤ 1 for each value, and (2) ΣP(X = x) = 1.
  • Expected value (mean): μ = E(X) = Σ[x · P(X = x)] — the long-run average.
  • Variance: σ² = Σ[(x − μ)² · P(X = x)]; Standard deviation: σ = √σ².
  • Examples: Number of heads in 3 coin flips, number of defective items in a batch, number rolled on a die.

flashcards.definition: A variable that takes a countable number of distinct numerical values based on random outcomes.
A table or function listing all possible values of a random variable and their probabilities, summing to 1.
The long-run average of a random variable: E(X) = Σ[x · P(X=x)].
σ² = Σ[(x − μ)² · P(X = x)]; measures spread of a probability distribution.
All probabilities must be between 0 and 1, and the sum of all probabilities must equal 1.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Continuous Random Variables
summary_sections.content_html:
Continuous Random Variables

A continuous random variable can take any value within an interval (e.g., height, weight, temperature).

  • Probability density function (PDF): f(x) describes the shape of the distribution. The total area under the curve = 1.
  • P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = area under the curve between a and b. For continuous variables, P(X = exact value) = 0.
  • Cumulative distribution function (CDF): F(x) = P(X ≤ x), the area under the curve from −∞ to x.
  • Mean (μ): The balance point of the distribution. Variance (σ²): Measures spread around the mean.
  • Uniform distribution: All values in [a, b] are equally likely. f(x) = 1/(b−a); E(X) = (a+b)/2.

Key difference from discrete: probability is found as an area, not a single point.

For a continuous random variable, the probability of any single exact value is 0.
F(x) = P(X ≤ x); the area under the PDF from −∞ to x.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Binomial Distribution
summary_sections.content_html:
Binomial Distribution

The binomial distribution models the number of successes in n independent trials, each with the same probability of success p.

Conditions (BINS):

  • Binary outcomes: each trial has exactly two outcomes (success/failure).
  • Independent trials: outcomes don't affect each other.
  • N is fixed: the number of trials is predetermined.
  • Same probability p for each trial.

Formula: P(X = k) = C(n,k) · pk · (1−p)n−k

Mean: μ = np   Variance: σ² = np(1−p)   Standard deviation: σ = √(np(1−p))

Example: Flipping a fair coin 10 times: n = 10, p = 0.5. P(exactly 3 heads) = C(10,3)(0.5)³(0.5)⁷ ≈ 0.117.

flashcards.definition: Models the number of successes in n independent trials with constant probability p: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1−p)^(n−k).
Binary outcomes, Independent trials, Number of trials is fixed, Same probability for each trial.
μ = np, where n is the number of trials and p is the probability of success.
σ² = np(1−p); standard deviation σ = √(np(1−p)).
The number of ways to choose k successes from n trials: C(n,k) = n!/[k!(n−k)!].

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Normal Distribution
summary_sections.content_html:
Normal Distribution

The normal distribution (Gaussian distribution) is the most important continuous distribution in statistics.

  • Bell-shaped and symmetric about the mean μ.
  • Fully described by two parameters: μ (mean/center) and σ (standard deviation/spread).
  • Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7): ~68% of data falls within 1σ of μ, ~95% within 2σ, ~99.7% within 3σ.
  • Standard normal distribution: μ = 0, σ = 1. Any normal variable X can be standardized: Z = (X − μ)/σ.
  • Z-table: Gives the area (probability) to the left of a given z-score.

Many natural phenomena approximate the normal distribution: heights, test scores, measurement errors, etc.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Central Limit Theorem
summary_sections.content_html:
Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

The Central Limit Theorem is one of the most important results in statistics.

Statement: For a population with mean μ and standard deviation σ, the sampling distribution of the sample mean x̄ approaches a normal distribution as n increases, regardless of the population's shape.

  • Mean of x̄: μx̄ = μ (the population mean).
  • Standard error: σx̄ = σ/√n (decreases as n increases).
  • Rule of thumb: n ≥ 30 is generally sufficient for the CLT to apply, though if the population is already normal, any n works.
  • Implication: Even if the population is skewed, x̄ will be approximately normal for large enough n.
  • Why it matters: The CLT justifies using z-scores and normal-based methods for inference with sample means.

flashcards.definition: The sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of population shape.
σ/√n; the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean.
The distribution of a statistic (like x̄) over all possible samples of the same size from a population.
A sample size of 30 or more is generally sufficient for the CLT to produce an approximately normal sampling distribution.
The mean of the sampling distribution of x̄ equals the population mean μ.

The distribution of these x̄ values is the sampling distribution

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Poisson Distribution
summary_sections.content_html:
Poisson Distribution

The Poisson distribution models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space when events happen independently at a constant average rate.

  • Parameter: λ (lambda) = the average number of events per interval.
  • Formula: P(X = k) = (e−λ · λk) / k!
  • Mean: E(X) = λ   Variance: Var(X) = λ   (mean equals variance!)
  • Conditions: Events occur independently, at a constant average rate, and cannot occur simultaneously.

Examples: Number of calls to a call center per hour, number of typos per page, number of car accidents at an intersection per month, number of emails received per day.

The Poisson can approximate the binomial when n is large and p is small (λ = np).

flashcards.definition: Models the count of events in a fixed interval when events occur independently at a constant rate λ.
The average rate of events per interval; in a Poisson distribution, both the mean and variance equal λ.
A unique property of the Poisson distribution: E(X) = Var(X) = λ.
When n is large and p is small, the binomial can be approximated by a Poisson with λ = np.

options.text: The average number of events per interval

quiz_questions.explanation: A unique feature: mean and variance are both equal to λ.

quiz_questions.explanation: The Poisson has a single parameter λ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Quality Control
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Quality Control

Statistical distributions are essential in quality control (QC) to monitor and improve manufacturing processes.

  • Control charts: Plot statistics (mean, proportion) over time to detect process deviations. Upper/lower control limits (UCL/LCL) are typically set at μ ± 3σ.
  • Process capability: Compares the spread of the process (6σ) to specification limits.
  • Acceptance sampling: Inspecting a sample from a batch to decide whether to accept or reject the lot. Uses binomial/Poisson distributions.
  • Defect rate: The proportion or count of defective items. Modeled by binomial (proportion) or Poisson (count per unit).
  • Six Sigma: A methodology aiming for ≤ 3.4 defects per million opportunities — about 6σ from the mean.

QC relies on sampling distributions and the CLT to make decisions about production quality from samples.

flashcards.definition: A graph plotting a process statistic over time with upper and lower control limits (UCL/LCL) to detect out-of-control processes.
Inspecting a random sample from a lot and deciding to accept or reject the entire lot based on the results.
A quality methodology targeting ≤ 3.4 defects per million opportunities; the process mean is 6 standard deviations from specifications.
Upper and Lower Control Limits, typically set at μ ± 3σ on a control chart.
A measure comparing the allowable spread (specification range) to the actual process spread (6σ).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Real-World Distributions
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Real-World Distributions

Statistical distributions appear throughout real life. Recognizing which distribution fits a situation is a key statistical skill.

  • Normal: Heights of adults, standardized test scores, measurement errors. Symmetric, bell-shaped data.
  • Binomial: Number of patients cured in n treated (fixed n, same p, independent). Pass/fail counts.
  • Poisson: Number of mutations in a DNA strand, number of accidents per month, calls per hour.
  • Uniform: Random number generators, equally likely outcomes in simulations.
  • Exponential: Time between arrivals, duration until a radioactive particle decays. Memoryless property.
  • Skewed distributions: Income distribution (right-skewed), wait times (often right-skewed).

Choosing the right model depends on the data type (discrete vs. continuous), shape, and underlying process.

flashcards.definition: Match the data characteristics (discrete/continuous, shape, process) to the appropriate probability model.
Models the time between independent events occurring at a constant rate; memoryless.
A distribution with a long tail to the right, such as income or housing prices.
A hypothesis test to determine whether observed data fits a specified probability distribution.
Using random number generation to model and study the behavior of complex systems.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sampling Methods (systematic, stratified, cluster)
summary_sections.content_html:
Sampling Methods

Good sampling is essential for valid statistical inference. Different methods suit different situations.

  • Simple Random Sample (SRS): Every individual has an equal chance of selection. Use a random number generator or table.
  • Systematic sampling: Select every k-th item from a list after a random start. Easy to implement but can be biased if the list has a periodic pattern.
  • Stratified sampling: Divide the population into homogeneous strata (e.g., age groups), then take an SRS from each stratum. Ensures representation of key subgroups.
  • Cluster sampling: Divide the population into clusters (e.g., schools, neighborhoods), randomly select some clusters, and sample ALL individuals in chosen clusters. Cost-effective for geographically spread populations.
  • Convenience sampling: Selecting whoever is easiest to reach — prone to bias; not recommended for formal studies.
  • Multi-stage sampling: Combines methods (e.g., cluster then SRS within clusters).

flashcards.definition: A sample where every individual in the population has an equal probability of being selected.
Dividing the population into homogeneous strata and taking an SRS from each to ensure subgroup representation.
Dividing the population into clusters, randomly selecting some clusters, and sampling all individuals within chosen clusters.
Selecting every k-th individual from a list after a random starting point.
Selecting individuals who are easiest to reach; highly prone to bias.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Bias in Sampling
summary_sections.content_html:
Bias in Sampling

Bias is a systematic error that causes sample results to consistently differ from the true population value.

  • Selection bias: Certain groups are systematically excluded from the sample (e.g., undercoverage, convenience sampling).
  • Response bias: Respondents give inaccurate answers due to question wording, social desirability, or misunderstanding.
  • Nonresponse bias: People who don't respond may differ systematically from those who do.
  • Voluntary response bias: Only strongly opinionated people choose to participate.
  • Leading questions: Questions worded to push respondents toward a particular answer.
  • Measurement bias: Faulty instruments or procedures consistently produce inaccurate measurements.

Bias cannot be eliminated by increasing sample size — only by improving methodology.

flashcards.definition: A systematic error that causes sample results to deviate from the true population value in a consistent direction.
Systematic exclusion of certain population groups from the sample, making the sample unrepresentative.
Occurs when individuals who don't respond differ systematically from those who do, skewing results.
Inaccurate answers caused by question wording, social desirability, or interviewer influence.

quiz_questions.explanation: A larger biased sample is still biased. Better methods are needed.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Designing Surveys & Experiments
summary_sections.content_html:
Designing Surveys & Experiments

Proper study design determines whether conclusions are valid.

  • Observational study: Researchers observe and measure without intervening. Can identify associations but NOT causation.
  • Experiment: Researchers actively impose treatments on subjects. Can establish cause and effect when properly designed.
  • Key elements of an experiment:
    • Treatment: The condition applied to experimental units.
    • Control group: Receives no treatment or a placebo for comparison.
    • Random assignment: Subjects are randomly assigned to treatment/control groups to balance confounding variables.
    • Replication: Using enough subjects for reliable results.
    • Blinding: Single-blind (subject doesn't know), double-blind (neither subject nor researcher knows).
  • Confounding variable: A variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, making it hard to determine the true cause.

flashcards.definition: A study where researchers observe subjects without imposing treatments; can show association but not causation.
A study where treatments are imposed on subjects; properly designed experiments can establish causation.
A variable related to both the explanatory and response variables that can distort the apparent relationship.
Randomly assigning subjects to treatment groups to balance confounding variables.
Neither the subjects nor the researchers measuring outcomes know which group receives the treatment.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Randomization & Control Groups
summary_sections.content_html:
Randomization & Control Groups

Randomization and control groups are the twin pillars of good experimental design.

  • Randomization: Assigning subjects to groups using a random process ensures that differences between groups are due to chance, not systematic factors.
  • Why randomize? Balances both known and unknown confounding variables across groups. Without randomization, groups may differ in ways that affect the outcome.
  • Control group: The group that does NOT receive the treatment, providing a baseline for comparison. May receive a placebo or standard treatment.
  • Types of control:
    • Placebo control: receives an inactive look-alike treatment.
    • Active control: receives the current standard treatment (common in medical trials).
    • No-treatment control: receives nothing (when ethically appropriate).
  • Statistical significance compares the observed effect to what could be expected by random chance alone.

flashcards.definition: Using a random process to assign subjects to treatment groups, balancing confounders across groups.
The comparison group that does not receive the experimental treatment.
A control group that receives an inactive treatment designed to look like the real treatment.
A control group that receives the current standard treatment for comparison to the new treatment.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Simulation Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Simulation Models

Simulation uses random number generation to model real-world processes and estimate probabilities or outcomes that are difficult to compute analytically.

  • Steps: (1) Define the model and assumptions. (2) Generate random outcomes using a random number generator. (3) Repeat many times (trials). (4) Analyze the distribution of results.
  • Monte Carlo simulation: A general method using repeated random sampling. Named after casino randomness.
  • Applications: Estimating probabilities, modeling financial risk, testing statistical methods, understanding complex systems.
  • Law of Large Numbers: As the number of simulation trials increases, the estimated probability approaches the true probability.
  • Random number tables and generators: Sources of random digits used to simulate chance processes.

Simulations provide approximate answers when exact calculation is impractical.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Political Polling
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Political Polling

Political polling is one of the most visible applications of sampling and statistics.

  • 1936 Literary Digest poll: Surveyed 2.4 million people by mail but predicted the wrong winner. Cause: selection bias (wealthy readers, voluntary response) despite huge sample size.
  • George Gallup correctly predicted the winner with just 50,000 responses using better sampling methods — proving that methodology matters more than size.
  • Margin of error: Polls report results like '52% ± 3%' — the margin of error reflects sampling variability.
  • Likely voter models: Pollsters screen for people likely to actually vote, not just all adults.
  • Response rates have declined significantly (from >35% to <6% for phone polls), raising concerns about nonresponse bias.
  • 2016 election polls: Underestimated support for certain candidates, partly due to late-deciding voters and difficulty reaching certain demographics.

The range within which the true population value likely falls; reflects sampling variability, typically ± a few percent.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ethics in Data Collection
summary_sections.content_html:
Ethics in Data Collection

Ethical considerations are fundamental to conducting responsible statistical research.

  • Informed consent: Participants must understand the study's purpose, procedures, risks, and benefits before voluntarily agreeing to participate.
  • Privacy and confidentiality: Personal data must be protected. Researchers should anonymize data when possible.
  • Institutional Review Board (IRB): A committee that reviews research involving human subjects to ensure ethical standards are met.
  • Do no harm: Research should minimize physical and psychological risks to participants.
  • Data integrity: Researchers must report data honestly — fabrication, falsification, and selective reporting are serious ethical violations.
  • Historical violations: The Tuskegee Syphilis Study (1932–1972) denied treatment to Black men without consent — a landmark case leading to modern ethical guidelines.
  • Vulnerable populations: Children, prisoners, and others with limited autonomy require extra protections.

flashcards.definition: A participant's voluntary agreement to participate after being fully informed about the study, its procedures, risks, and benefits.
A committee that reviews and approves research involving human subjects to ensure ethical standards.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Confidence Intervals (mean, proportion)
summary_sections.content_html:
Confidence Intervals

A confidence interval (CI) provides a range of plausible values for a population parameter based on sample data.

General form: point estimate ± margin of error

  • CI for a mean (σ known): x̄ ± z* · (σ/√n)
  • CI for a mean (σ unknown): x̄ ± t* · (s/√n) using the t-distribution with n−1 degrees of freedom.
  • CI for a proportion: p̂ ± z* · √(p̂(1−p̂)/n)
  • Confidence level (e.g., 95%): In repeated sampling, 95% of such intervals would contain the true parameter.
  • Common z* values: 90% → 1.645, 95% → 1.960, 99% → 2.576.
  • Conditions: Random sample, independence, normality (or large n via CLT), and for proportions: np̂ ≥ 10 and n(1−p̂) ≥ 10.

flashcards.definition: A range of values (point estimate ± margin of error) likely to contain the true population parameter at a given confidence level.
The proportion of intervals (e.g., 95%) that would contain the true parameter in repeated sampling.
The maximum expected difference between the sample statistic and the true parameter; half the width of the CI.
A bell-shaped distribution used when σ is unknown; wider than normal, with n−1 degrees of freedom.
Random sample, independence, and normality checks (or large n); for proportions: np̂ ≥ 10 and n(1−p̂) ≥ 10.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Margin of Error
summary_sections.content_html:
Margin of Error

The margin of error (ME) quantifies the precision of a sample estimate.

  • For a mean: ME = z* · (σ/√n) or t* · (s/√n).
  • For a proportion: ME = z* · √(p̂(1−p̂)/n).
  • Interpretation: We are [confidence level]% confident that the true parameter is within ME of our estimate.

Factors affecting ME:

  • ↑ Confidence level → ↑ ME (wider interval).
  • ↑ Sample size (n) → ↓ ME (more precise).
  • ↑ Variability (σ or s) → ↑ ME (less precise).

Sample size determination: To achieve a desired ME, solve for n. For a proportion: n = (z*/ME)² · p̂(1−p̂). When p̂ is unknown, use p̂ = 0.5 (maximum variability, most conservative).

flashcards.definition: The maximum expected distance between the sample estimate and the true population parameter.
Higher confidence level, smaller sample size, and greater variability all increase the margin of error.
n = (z*/ME)² · p̂(1−p̂) for proportions; use p̂ = 0.5 when the proportion is unknown.
A smaller margin of error means greater precision — the estimate is closer to the true value.
Using p̂ = 0.5 when planning sample size gives the largest required n, ensuring adequate precision.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hypothesis Testing (null vs. alternative)
summary_sections.content_html:
Hypothesis Testing

Hypothesis testing is a formal procedure for using data to evaluate a claim about a population parameter.

  • Null hypothesis (H₀): The default claim — often 'no effect' or 'no difference.' Assumed true until evidence suggests otherwise.
  • Alternative hypothesis (Hₐ): The claim that contradicts H₀ — what the researcher believes or wants to show.
  • Test statistic: A z-score or t-score computed from the sample data measuring how far the sample result is from H₀.
  • P-value: The probability of obtaining results as extreme (or more extreme) than observed, assuming H₀ is true.
  • Decision: If p-value ≤ α, reject H₀ (statistically significant). If p-value > α, fail to reject H₀.
  • Significance level (α): The threshold (usually 0.05) for deciding statistical significance.

Steps: (1) State H₀ and Hₐ. (2) Choose α. (3) Compute the test statistic. (4) Find the p-value. (5) Make a decision and state a conclusion in context.

flashcards.definition: The default claim of no effect or no difference; assumed true until evidence suggests otherwise.
The claim the researcher wants to support; contradicts H₀.
The probability of observing results as extreme or more extreme than the sample, assuming H₀ is true.
The threshold for rejecting H₀; commonly set at 0.05.
A standardized value (z or t) measuring how far the sample result is from what H₀ predicts.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Type I & Type II Errors
summary_sections.content_html:
Type I & Type II Errors

When making decisions based on hypothesis tests, two types of errors can occur.

  • Type I error (false positive): Rejecting H₀ when H₀ is actually true. Probability = α (significance level).
  • Type II error (false negative): Failing to reject H₀ when H₀ is actually false (Hₐ is true). Probability = β.
  • Power = 1 − β: The probability of correctly rejecting a false H₀. Higher power = better ability to detect a real effect.

Trade-offs:

  • Decreasing α → decreases Type I error but increases Type II error (harder to reject H₀).
  • Increasing sample size → increases power (reduces β) without changing α.
  • Larger true effect size → easier to detect → higher power.
H₀ TrueH₀ False
Reject H₀Type I error (α)Correct (Power = 1−β)
Fail to reject H₀CorrectType II error (β)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: P-values & Significance Levels
summary_sections.content_html:
P-values & Significance Levels

The p-value is the probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme (or more extreme) than the one observed, assuming H₀ is true.

  • Small p-value: The observed data is unlikely under H₀ → evidence against H₀.
  • Large p-value: The observed data is consistent with H₀ → insufficient evidence against H₀.
  • Decision rule: If p-value ≤ α, reject H₀. If p-value > α, fail to reject H₀.

Common misconceptions:

  • The p-value is NOT the probability that H₀ is true.
  • The p-value is NOT the probability of making an error.
  • A statistically significant result is not necessarily practically important.
  • A non-significant result does not prove H₀ is true.

Effect size and confidence intervals should accompany p-values to provide a fuller picture.

flashcards.definition: The probability of observing data as extreme or more extreme than what was observed, assuming H₀ is true.
The pre-set threshold for p-value comparison; typically 0.05. If p ≤ α, reject H₀.
A result can be statistically significant (small p) but not practically meaningful (small effect).
The p-value is NOT the probability that H₀ is true—it's the probability of the data given H₀.
A measure of the magnitude of a difference or relationship, complementing the p-value.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Interpreting Statistical Results
summary_sections.content_html:
Interpreting Statistical Results

Correct interpretation of statistical results is just as important as the calculations themselves.

  • Context: Always state conclusions in the context of the problem, not just 'reject H₀.'
  • Statistical vs. practical significance: A statistically significant result (small p) may have a trivially small effect. Always check effect size.
  • Confidence interval interpretation: 'We are 95% confident that the true [parameter] is between [a] and [b].'
  • Association vs. causation: Observational studies can show association but not cause-and-effect. Only randomized experiments establish causation.
  • Generalizability: Results generalize only to the population from which the sample was drawn.
  • Limitations: Acknowledge assumptions, potential biases, and the scope of conclusions.

Common mistakes: Confusing correlation with causation, misinterpreting p-values, ignoring assumptions, and overgeneralizing results.

flashcards.definition: Stating statistical conclusions in terms of the real-world problem, not just statistical jargon.
Association (correlation) means variables are related; causation means one variable causes changes in another.
Whether a statistically significant result is large enough to matter in the real world.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Medical Trials
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Medical Trials

Medical trials (clinical trials) are among the most rigorous applications of statistical inference.

  • Phases:
    • Phase I: Safety and dosage (small group).
    • Phase II: Efficacy and side effects (larger group).
    • Phase III: Compared to standard treatment or placebo (large RCT).
    • Phase IV: Post-market surveillance.
  • Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs): The gold standard. Random assignment + control group + blinding.
  • Intention-to-treat (ITT): Analyzing participants in their originally assigned groups regardless of compliance — prevents bias.
  • Key examples:
    • Salk polio vaccine trial (1954): 1.8 million children, double-blind, proved vaccine efficacy.
    • Framingham Heart Study: Long-running observational study identifying heart disease risk factors.
    • COVID-19 vaccine trials: Phase III RCTs with 30,000+ participants establishing safety and efficacy.

flashcards.definition: Phase I (safety), Phase II (efficacy), Phase III (large RCT comparison), Phase IV (post-market surveillance).
The gold standard study design using random assignment, control groups, and often blinding.
Analyzing all participants in their originally assigned groups regardless of adherence to maintain randomization benefits.
Neither participants nor researchers know group assignments, minimizing bias from expectations.
Improvement in patients receiving an inactive treatment, due to their belief that they are receiving real therapy.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Scatterplots & Correlation Coefficient
summary_sections.content_html:
Scatterplots & Correlation Coefficient

A scatterplot displays the relationship between two quantitative variables, with each point representing an observation.

  • Direction: Positive (up-right), negative (down-right), or no trend.
  • Form: Linear, curved, or no clear pattern.
  • Strength: How tightly the points follow the pattern (strong, moderate, weak).
  • Correlation coefficient (r): Measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables.
  • Properties of r: −1 ≤ r ≤ 1. r = 1 means perfect positive linear; r = −1 means perfect negative linear; r = 0 means no linear relationship.
  • r only measures linear relationships — a strong curved pattern can have r ≈ 0.
  • r is unitless and not affected by changes in units or which variable is x vs. y.
  • Outliers can strongly influence r.

flashcards.definition: A graph displaying the relationship between two quantitative variables using points in the coordinate plane.
A value between −1 and 1 measuring the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Least Squares Regression Line
summary_sections.content_html:
Least Squares Regression Line (LSRL)

The least squares regression line (LSRL) is the line that minimizes the sum of the squared vertical distances (residuals) between the data points and the line.

Equation: ŷ = a + bx, where:

  • b = r(sy/sx) — the slope.
  • a = ȳ − bx̄ — the y-intercept.
  • The line always passes through the point (x̄, ȳ).

Interpreting slope (b): For each one-unit increase in x, ŷ changes by b units on average.

Interpreting intercept (a): The predicted y-value when x = 0 (may not be meaningful in context).

r² (coefficient of determination): The proportion of variation in y explained by the linear model. r² = 0.75 means 75% of the variability in y is explained by x.

Extrapolation: Predicting y for x values far outside the data range — unreliable and should be avoided.

flashcards.definition: The line ŷ = a + bx that minimizes the sum of squared residuals.
The change in predicted y (ŷ) for each one-unit increase in x; b = r(sᵧ/sₓ).
The predicted value of y when x = 0; a = ȳ − bx̄.
The proportion of variability in y that is explained by the linear relationship with x.
Using the regression line to predict y for x values far outside the range of the data; unreliable.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Residuals & Line Fit
summary_sections.content_html:
Residuals & Line Fit

A residual is the difference between an observed y-value and the predicted ŷ-value: residual = y − ŷ.

  • Residual plot: A scatterplot of residuals vs. x (or vs. ŷ). Used to assess whether the linear model is appropriate.
  • Good fit: Residuals are randomly scattered around 0 with no clear pattern.
  • Problems: A curved pattern in the residual plot suggests a nonlinear relationship; a fan shape suggests non-constant variance (heteroscedasticity).
  • Standard deviation of residuals (s): Measures the typical size of prediction errors: s = √[Σ(y − ŷ)²/(n − 2)].
  • Influential points: Points with extreme x-values (high leverage) that can dramatically change the regression line.
  • Outliers in regression: Points with large residuals that don't fit the pattern.

flashcards.definition: The difference between the observed and predicted values: residual = y − ŷ.
A scatterplot of residuals vs. x-values used to check whether the linear model is appropriate.
Random scatter around 0 with no systematic pattern — indicates the linear model fits well.
A point (often with extreme x) that has a large effect on the regression line.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Multiple Regression (introductory)
summary_sections.content_html:
Multiple Regression (Introduction)

Multiple regression extends simple linear regression to include two or more explanatory variables.

Model: ŷ = b₀ + b₁x₁ + b₂x₂ + … + bₖxₖ

  • b₀: The intercept (predicted y when all x's = 0).
  • bᵢ: The predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in xᵢ, holding all other variables constant.
  • R² (multiple): The proportion of variation in y explained by ALL the predictors combined. R² always increases when more predictors are added.
  • Adjusted R²: Penalizes for the number of predictors; only increases if a new variable genuinely improves the model.
  • Multicollinearity: When predictor variables are highly correlated with each other, making individual coefficients unreliable.

Multiple regression is widely used in science, business, economics, and social sciences to model complex relationships.

flashcards.definition: A regression model with two or more explanatory variables: ŷ = b₀ + b₁x₁ + b₂x₂ + … + bₖxₖ.
Each coefficient bᵢ represents the effect of xᵢ while other predictors are held fixed.
A modified R² that penalizes for additional predictors; prevents overfitting.
When predictor variables are highly correlated with each other, making individual coefficients unreliable.
Including too many predictors that capture noise rather than true patterns, reducing prediction quality on new data.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nonlinear Regression Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Nonlinear Regression Models

When the relationship between x and y is not linear, nonlinear models may fit better.

  • Quadratic model: ŷ = a + bx + cx². Used for U-shaped or inverted-U patterns.
  • Exponential model: ŷ = abx. Used for growth/decay patterns. Taking log(y) can linearize: log(ŷ) = log(a) + x·log(b).
  • Power model: ŷ = axb. Taking logs of both sides: log(ŷ) = log(a) + b·log(x).
  • Logarithmic model: ŷ = a + b·ln(x). Used when growth slows over time.
  • Transformations: Taking log, square root, or reciprocal of x or y to straighten a curved relationship so linear regression can be applied.

Choosing a model: Examine the scatterplot, try transformations, check residual plots, and compare r² values of different models.
